nativeRange.habitat: Soft, acidic blackwater rivers and forest streams; benthopelagic in shallow, heavily tannin-stained waters with submerged roots, leaf litter, and minimal current

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java moss, floating plants, fine-leaved stems)
dried leaf litter (Indian almond, oak) to release tannins and lower pH
driftwood and twisted roots for cover and tannin release
dim or diffused lighting to mimic blackwater conditions

diet.notes: Micro-predator in the wild. Accepts small live and frozen foods: Artemia nauplii, micro worms, daphnia, sieved cyclops, and bloodworms. Live foods strongly recommended during acclimation to encourage feeding. Once settled, will accept high-quality micro pellets and crushed flakes, but variety with frozen and live foods is important for long-term health.

goodWith: small blackwater tetras (e.g., cardinal tetra, ember tetra)
small rasboras tolerant of soft-acid water
dwarf cichlids adapted to blackwater (e.g., Apistogramma spp. with caution)
celestial pearl danios in soft-water setups
avoidWith: large or boisterous fish that will outcompete them for food
hard-water fish incompatible with pH below 6

summary: A rare, miniature Rio Negro blackwater tetra demanding strict soft-acid conditions (pH 4.5–6.0). Males bear unique bony anal-fin hooks used to clasp females during internal insemination — an unusual reproductive strategy among characids.
careNotes:
Brittanichthys axelrodi is a miniature blackwater tetra (max ~4 cm) from the soft, highly acidic, tannin-rich rivers of the Rio Negro basin in Brazil and Colombia, including the Rio Urubani, Rio Demini, and Rio Muco. It is rarely encountered in the hobby and is considered an advanced-level fish due to strict water-chemistry requirements and sensitivity to suboptimal conditions.

Water quality is paramount: this species demands very soft (gH 0–4), near-acidic to acidic water with pH 4.5–6.0 and temperatures of 22–28°C. Tap water is typically unsuitable; reverse-osmosis or deionised water reconstituted with small amounts of mineral salts, or peat-filtered rainwater, is the recommended approach. Peat filtration in a mature aquarium further assists with maintaining stable low-pH conditions and provides mild bacteriostatic benefit. Nitrates must be kept very low (below 25 mg/L); frequent partial water changes using pre-conditioned soft water are essential.

A minimum group of 10 individuals is strongly advised — too small a group leads to stress, shyness, and increased disease susceptibility. A heavily planted aquarium with dried leaf litter (Indian almond leaves, oak leaves), driftwood, and subdued lighting replicates the blackwater biotope and helps fish settle. A tank of at least 60 L with a 60 cm footprint is suitable for a group of 10–12.

During acclimation, live Artemia nauplii, micro worms, and sieved pond food are the best foods to offer; frozen foods are also accepted. Dry foods may be accepted once settled. The species is sensitive to sudden parameter shifts; slow drip acclimation is recommended when introducing new fish.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is very rarely achieved and poorly documented. This species is an internal inseminator — unlike most tetras which scatter eggs and fertilise externally, males transfer spermatozoa directly into the female’s reproductive tract. Males possess distinctive bony hooks on the fourth unbranched anal-fin ray and branched rays 1–4, which are used to clasp females during courtship and sperm transfer. The testis morphology is unique among characids, featuring a tripartite structure that produces and stores spermatozoa and forms spermatozeugmata (unencapsulated sperm packets). A successful captive breeding account was published by Peter and Martin Hoffmann in the German magazine DATZ (Issue 3/2010, p. 18). Strict blackwater conditions and a species-specific setup are likely prerequisites for any breeding attempt.

primary.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Brittanichthys axelrodi
primary.notes: Primary trade/hobbyist source. Confirmed blackwater specialist status, water parameters (pH 4.5–6, low hardness, 22–28°C), diet (live foods during acclimation, frozen and dry foods once settled), group size recommendation (minimum 10), and breeding notes referencing the DATZ 3/2010 Hoffmann report.

additional.name: FishBase — Brittanichthys axelrodi
additional.notes: Confirmed max size 2.8 cm SL (male/unsexed), native range (Negro River basin, South America), taxonomy (Characiformes), IUCN Least Concern status (2018), and benthopelagic freshwater tropical habitat classification.

additional.name: PubMed — Reproductive morphology of Brittanichthys axelrodi (Teleostei: Characidae)
additional.notes: Peer-reviewed study confirming internal insemination, bony hooks on fourth unbranched anal-fin ray and branched rays 1–4, unique tripartite testis morphology, and presence of spermatozoa in female ovaries. Established reproductive mode as insemination rather than external fertilisation.

additional.name: Aquarium Glaser GmbH — Brittanichthys axelrodi
additional.notes: Trade importer notes confirming rarity in trade, blackwater water chemistry requirements (pH 4.5–6, peat filtration), diet (live Artemia nauplii, sieved pond food, frozen food), max size ~4 cm, and reference to DATZ breeding report. Also noted males have glandular scales in caudal fin region.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Brittanichthys
additional.notes: Confirmed genus taxonomy (Characidae, subfamily Megalamphodinae, Characiformes), established by Jacques Géry 1965, two valid species (B. axelrodi and B. myersi), endemism to Rio Negro basin, and etymology (genus honors M.R. Brittan; species epithet honors Herbert R. Axelrod whose T.F.H. Fund sponsored the type-specimen expedition).

fish.breedingNotes: Internal inseminator; males use bony anal-fin hooks to clasp females during sperm transfer. Tripartite testis morphology unique among characids. Captive breeding extremely rare; strict blackwater conditions required. Only one published hobbyist breeding account known (DATZ 3/2010).
